Heat pumps cool our homes better and hold a steady temperature to keep your home cool all summer. Because they run on electricity, heat pumps cool AND heat your home way more efficiently.​ Experts estimate that heat pumps can cut climate pollution from buildings by 40% or more.

UNFAIR TRIAL: A billionaire oil CEO is trying to silence environmental activists forever. Kelcy Warren, the CEO who made billions from the Dakota Access Pipeline, has filed a staggering $300 million lawsuit against Greenpeace. Why? Because they stood with Indigenous water protectors at Standing Rock. This isn't just another lawsuit. It's a calculated attack to bankrupt one of the world's most effective environmental organizations and send a chilling message to anyone who dares challenge Big Oil. Warren – who once said environmental protesters "need to be removed from the gene pool" – chose a remote North Dakota courtroom for this showdown. That's why human rights lawyer Steven Donziger is taking action. He experienced similar tactics when Chevron targeted him after he won a pollution case against them. Steven is bringing a team of renowned civil rights attorneys to monitor this trial including: Marty Garbus (former lawyer for Nelson Mandela) Natali Segovia (Director, Water Protector Legal Collective) Jeanne Mirer (President, National Association of Democratic Lawyers) We need to raise $100,000 for legal monitoring and a global campaign to expose this billionaire's attempt to crush  …

Trey Cox is leading the legal team representing Kelcy Warren’s company in a case that could shut down Greenpeace. The $669 million judgment, tied to Greenpeace’s support for Indigenous-led protest against a pipeline, could devastate one of the world’s most effective environmental groups. Now, Cox’s team is asking the court to require Greenpeace to post a $75 million bond just to file an appeal. If approved, that bond could spell the end of Greenpeace’s ability to defend itself in court. It’s a move that would send a chilling message to anyone standing up to corporate power. This case mirrors a pattern we’ve seen before: powerful interests turning to the courts to overwhelm environmental defenders with massive legal costs. Greenpeace is fighting back — from challenging the bond to filing a case in Europe under new anti-SLAPP laws. But they can’t do it alone. If you believe in the right to protest, to speak out, and to protect our planet — now’s the time to act. Get behind Greenpeace so they can win this appeal 👉https://chuffed.org/campaign/help-stop-big-oil/20250619a1 Comment WITH YOU when you have donated

We’ve seen this before. People hauled away by ICE without warrants. Protesters arrested for speaking out. Legal threats used to chill dissent. Now the fossil fuel industry is copying that playbook — and using the courts to do it. Greenpeace is facing a massive judgment and a demand to post $75 million just to keep their case alive. It’s a new kind of suppression — legal, polished, and devastating. On July 2, a major hearing will decide what happens next. And if no one’s watching, the message will be clear: protest has a price tag. That’s why we’re acting fast. We’re raising funds to support legal observers, public reporting, and a full investigation into what this case means for protest rights — here and globally. This isn’t just a courtroom drama. It’s a warning shot aimed at everyone fighting for justice.

Kandi Mossett White stood on the land where her ancestors were buried and watched as Energy Transfer bulldozed their graves to make way for the Dakota Access Pipeline. She saw it with her own eyes. The destruction wasn’t “alleged”—it happened. Yet now, a North Dakota jury has ruled in favor of Energy Transfer, awarding them millions in a defamation lawsuit against Greenpeace for saying exactly that. This isn’t justice. This is corporate power using the legal system to rewrite history. Greenpeace stood with the Standing Rock Sioux to defend sacred land and water. Now they’re being punished for telling the truth. This case isn’t just about Greenpeace—it’s about silencing anyone who dares to challenge the fossil fuel industry’s destruction of Indigenous lands. If this ruling stands, it could bankrupt one of the world’s leading environmental defenders and set a terrifying precedent for corporate attacks on activists. Renowned human rights lawyer Steven Donziger—who helped hold Chevron accountable for poisoning Indigenous communities in the Amazon—is fighting back. His team is exposing the deep flaws in this case and mobilizing to defend Greenpeace from this blatant abuse of power. “Thi …

"The fossil fuel industry wants this buried. In a North Dakota courtroom, a pipeline giant used a lawsuit to try to crush Greenpeace—and by extension, the entire climate justice and Indigenous rights movements. What we saw in that courtroom was a travesty of justice. Jurors openly admitted bias. Some worked in the fossil fuel industry. There was no court reporter. No public transcript. And the judge lost control. We formed a trial monitoring team to document what no one else would. Our notes are the only public record of what happened. And now, we need your help to release a full report, launch a UN complaint, and keep monitoring what happens next. This isn’t just about Greenpeace. It’s about protecting the right to protest—and stopping corporations from rewriting the rules to silence movements that fight for people and the planet." We’re raising $100,000 to fight back. Please donate what you can.

Carrboro officials filed a landmark lawsuit Wednesday afternoon against Duke Energy Corp., accusing the utility giant of orchestrating a decades-long climate deception campaign. The suit, filed in Orange County Superior Court, claims Duke Energy misled the public about fossil fuels’ role in the climate crisis, delaying a transition to renewable energy and costing the town millions. At a press conference, Mayor Barbara Foushee called the move a stand against environmental injustice. “Historically underserved and marginalized communities are facing disproportionate impacts and health risks that are associated with climate change,” Foushee said. “This was not an easy decision to make, but I believe that we must be courageous as we call out these injustices and seek change and accountability.” The lawsuit highlights Duke Energy’s alleged strategy to block climate action while profiting from fossil fuels, despite knowing their dangers for over 50 years. Mayor Pro Tem Danny Nowell said, “It’s time for us to hold Duke Energy accountable for decades of deception, padding executives’ pockets while towns like ours worked to mitigate the harmful effects of climate change. This suit will allow …

From the desks of @polluterspay & @public_citizen: "Climate change is already harming people in communities across Rhode Island. In Westerly and Narragansett, homeowners face skyrocketing flood insurance premiums. In Newport, historic sites require multimillion-dollar protections from rising seas. These mounting costs are falling disproportionately on taxpayers, small businesses, and vulnerable communities, yet they are in large part the result of reckless conduct undertaken by major fossil fuel companies that are responsible for generating the vast majority of all the global greenhouse gas emissions that have caused our planet to heat up. That’s not fair. The companies that created this mess should help pay to clean it up. That’s exactly what the Rhode Island Climate Superfund Act...would require — that Big Oil companies help our state adapt to the climate crisis they knowingly caused, in an amount commensurate with the proportion of overall global emissions they are responsible for generating."

5 ways Big Oil spread climate change misinformation: 1. In 1978, Exxon admitted that the “present trend of fossil fuels will cause dramatic environmental effects by 2050.” Later, Exxon lobbied to convince the public that climate change science is uncertain. 2. Multiple oil companies pledged to be net zero and align with the Paris Climate Agreement. Internally, BP said “No one is committed to anything other to stay in the game.” 3. The fossil fuel industry has rebranded methane gas to “natural gas.” Although BP publicly marketed gas as clean, internally, they knew methane emissions do not support climate goals. 4. In 2008, oil companies committed to develop algae as a biofuel. Exxon spent $175 million to advertise their research. Their research lagged and by 2023, all companies ended their algae programs. 5. Six fossil fuel companies spent $700 million on academic research programs, leading to public research favoring methane gas over renewable energy. Schools include Harvard, Princeton, Tufts, MIT, and Berkeley. Big Oil lied for decades. And our campaign continues to call them out. #TheyKnewTheyLied

Cutting methane pollution is still the fastest way to reduce warming in the short term. We need ambitious action to seize this opportunity.

Here’s what most people don’t know: 1. Natural gas is made up of mostly methane. 2. Methane pollution is causing the planet to heat up faster than carbon dioxide would alone. 3. Methane is bad for our health. The good news? There are already solutions that can make a difference today.

Methane leaks are like an invisible climate change bomb blowing up around us all the time: Methane is 86x more potent at warming our planet than carbon dioxide and is responsible for at least 30% of global temperature rises. But most methane emissions are just preventable leaks caused by carelessness and poor maintenance by oil and gas companies, from storage tanks, pipelines, and pumping stations. Your governor can take action now to reduce or eliminate these preventable methane emissions in your state, by implementing basic EPA rules. Add your voice and tell your governor to take action on your state’s methane policy today.

🌿 3 Ways You Can Help Protect Mangroves 🌊 Mangroves are essential to the health of the Gulf of Mexico, and you can make a difference in preserving these vital ecosystems. Here are the three actions you can take: Secure your trash. Because mangroves sit at the edge of the sea, they often become a trap for marine debris. Make sure your trash on and off the water is secure and bring an extra container to collect trash when you see it. Reduce Runoff and Pollution. Minimize the use of fertilizers and chemicals in your yard. Runoff from these substances can harm mangrove habitats and the marine life that depends on them. Opt for natural alternatives and properly dispose of hazardous waste. Support Local Conservation Efforts. Get involved with or donate to organizations working to protect and restore mangrove forests. Your support helps fund crucial research and conservation projects that safeguard these ecosystems. Every action counts when it comes to protecting our mangroves and the vital role they play in the Gulf of Mexico. Wildfires in the United States are becoming more frequent and destructive, fueled by the growing impacts of global warming and climate change. Rising temperatures, prolonged droughts, and shifting weather patterns have created the perfect conditions for fires to spread faster and burn longer, devastating communities and natural landscapes. In recent years, wildfires have caused unprecedented damage to homes, wildlife, and infrastructure while also contributing to worsening air quality and health risks. Over the past 40 years, the average number of acres of forested land consumed by wildfire each year in the United States has increased by 1,000%, according to National Geographic. The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) reports wildfires caused $81.6 billion in damage from 2017 to 2021, a nearly 10-fold increase from 2012 to 2016 when damages totaled $8.6 billion. A report in March 2022 by the American Association for the Advancement of Science revealed a concerning trend in fires that occurred in the past 13 years compared to the previous 20 years. Notably, on the West and East coasts, fire frequency doubled, underscoring the regional impact of this issue. In the  …

🚨 Food waste isn’t just waste—it’s a methane crisis! 🚨 ReFED’s 2025 U.S. Food Waste Report reveals that food waste remains a major contributor to climate change, emitting methane as it rots in landfills. At Energy Vision, our Meeting the Methane Challenge report lays out a clear, actionable path: ✅ Edible food should be redistributed to those in need. ✅ Inedible food should be diverted to anaerobic digesters (ADs) to prevent methane emissions and generate renewable energy. If we act now, we can cut U.S. methane emissions by 13.6% annually—a major step toward the Global Methane Pledge of 30% reductions by 2030. The future is clear: No food in landfills. The Impact of Climate Change on Your Health: What You Need to Know Published on: Jan 25, 2025 WRITTEN BY Emily Beyda https://masterspublichealth.com/climate-change-impact-on-your-health/ Key Takeaways Climate change has multifaceted and profound impacts on human health, ranging from vector-borne diseases to respiratory diseases. Rising temperatures, increased air pollution, and extreme weather events all exacerbate health conditions, disproportionately impacting vulnerable populations. Global efforts to mitigate the impact of climate change may be able to reduce its impacts on human health. When we think of climate change, we tend to focus on its environmental impacts, but climate change is also a significant contributor to the global health crisis. Climate change and public health are closely linked by effects of global warming like shifting global temperatures, diminished air quality, extreme weather events, and altered ecosystems. All of these concerns may be linked to environmental health, but directly impact human health, especially when it comes to health equity. It might be assumed that the Pope's message was met with universal acceptance. However, Pope Francis's environmental views drew sharp criticism from some within the Catholic Church as well as from anti-environmentalists outside the church. Apparently, a message that placed blame on human greed, lifestyles of excessive consumption, and governments that were unwilling or afraid to rein in environmental degradation was not what certain people wanted to hear. Quotes below: A selfish and boundless thirst for power and material prosperity leads both to the misuse of available natural resources and to the exclusion of the weak and disadvantaged. We must not be indifferent or resigned to the loss of biodiversity and the destruction of ecosystems, often caused by our irresponsible and selfish behavior... Because of us, thousands of species will no …

The passing of Pope Francis is a loss for those who believe in justice, humility, and the courage to speak truth to power. He didn’t lead with fear or hierarchy; he led with compassion, he stood with the poor, the displaced, the forgotten. He challenged political systems and, just as boldly, challenged the structures within his own Church. He was one of the few global leaders who treated climate change as a moral emergency. According to the New York Times, in 2015, he issued Laudato Si, the first papal encyclical focused entirely on the environment. He connected the climate crisis to poverty, inequality, and human suffering, long before most world leaders had the courage to do so. And he didn’t hesitate to speak out, in his own way, against world leaders who commit crimes and violate human rights. He condemned war, authoritarianism, and indifference to suffering, using his voice to remind us that silence in the face of injustice is never an option. Pope Francis’ legacy reaches far beyond religion. Voices like his are rare! We should embrace every word he uttered. #PopeFrancis
